subject: Islamiyat
chapter_name: Articles of Faith and Pillars of Islam

note_data.title: Articles of Faith and Pillars of Islam
keyPoints: **Shahadah** is the declaration of faith stating 'There is no god but Allah and Muhammad is His messenger'
**Salah** is the five daily prayers performed at Fajr, Dhuhr, Asr, Maghrib and Isha
**Zakat** is the obligatory charity of 2.5% of wealth given to eight categories of recipients
**Sawm** is fasting during the month of Ramadan from dawn to sunset
**Hajj** is the pilgrimage to Makkah that must be performed once in a lifetime if physically and financially able
**Iman** encompasses belief in Allah, angels, revealed books, prophets, Day of Judgment and predestination
The **Five Pillars** are the foundation of Muslim life and worship
Belief in **Tawhid** (oneness of Allah) is the central principle of Islamic faith
The **Six Articles of Faith** form the basis of Islamic belief system

definitions.definition: The declaration of faith stating there is no god but Allah and Muhammad is His messenger
The five daily prayers that Muslims must perform facing Makkah
Obligatory charity of 2.5% of wealth given annually to the poor and needy
The pilgrimage to Makkah that every Muslim must perform once if able
Faith or belief in the six articles of Islamic faith

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give details about how something is done or what it involves
Give the main features or key points in logical order

modelAnswer.question: Explain the importance of Salah for Muslims. [4 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Salah is important because it is the second pillar of Islam and a direct command from Allah. It helps Muslims maintain a constant connection with Allah throughout the day. Salah purifies the soul from sins and keeps Muslims away from evil deeds. It also unites the Muslim community as they all pray facing the same direction towards the Kaaba.

commonMistakes: Never say 'Muhammad is God' — always say 'Muhammad is the messenger of Allah'
Students often confuse Zakat with Sadaqah — Zakat is obligatory, Sadaqah is voluntary
Don't say Hajj is performed 'when convenient' — it must be done when physically and financially able
Avoid saying 'five pillars of faith' — they are pillars of Islam, articles of faith are separate
Never write that fasting is only about not eating — include not drinking and sexual relations
examinerTips: This topic appears in every Paper 2 examination
Always give specific examples when explaining the pillars
Learn the Arabic terms as they often appear in questions
Connect pillars to their spiritual and social benefits for full marks
Remember to mention Quran and Hadith references where relevant
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Five-pointed diagram showing the Five Pillars with Shahadah at the center, surrounded by Salah, Zakat, Sawm, and Hajj, often used to illustrate the foundation of Islamic practice
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P1 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain three of the Six Articles of Faith and their importance in a Muslim's life
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q2(a) (4 marks) — candidates had to describe what Muslims believe about the Day of Judgment as an Article of Faith
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P1 Q3(b) (8 marks) — students needed to explain why the Five Pillars are considered the foundation of Islamic practice
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P2 Q1(c) (6 marks) — they asked for detailed explanation of Hajj as one of the Five Pillars with its spiritual significance

workedExample.question: Explain the importance of Salah (prayer) as one of the Five Pillars of Islam. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Define Salah as the second Pillar of Islam - the five daily prayers
Step 2: Explain the spiritual importance - direct connection with Allah, remembrance throughout the day
Step 3: Describe the community aspect - praying together at mosque builds unity (ummah)
Step 4: Mention the disciplinary aspect - creates routine, self-control, and God-consciousness (taqwa)
workedExample.answer: Salah is the second Pillar of Islam, consisting of five daily prayers performed at specific times. It is fundamentally important because it establishes a direct spiritual connection between the Muslim and Allah, ensuring constant remembrance of God throughout the day. The communal aspect of prayer, especially Friday prayers at the mosque, strengthens the bond of brotherhood (ummah) among Muslims. Additionally, Salah develops self-discipline and God-consciousness (taqwa), as Muslims must stop their daily activities to fulfill this obligation, demonstrating their submission to Allah's will.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for identifying Salah as daily prayers, 2 marks for spiritual significance, 2 marks for community/discipline aspects, 1 mark for use of Arabic terminology

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the Six Articles of Faith with the Five Pillars' — always clearly distinguish between beliefs (Articles) and practices (Pillars) when answering questions
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often listed the Pillars without explaining their significance' — always provide detailed explanation of WHY each Pillar/Article is important, not just WHAT it is

Examiner Report 2023: 'Weak responses failed to use appropriate Islamic terminology' — always include Arabic terms like taqwa, ummah, shirk, and akhirah with brief explanations to demonstrate understanding
note_data.keyDiagram: Students should be able to draw two interconnected circles: one labeled 'Six Articles of Faith (Beliefs)' containing Tawhid, Angels, Holy Books, Prophets, Akhirah, and Predestination; the other labeled 'Five Pillars of Islam (Practices)' containing Shahadah, Salah, Zakah, Sawm, and Hajj. Draw arrows connecting them to show how beliefs motivate practices, with a caption explaining that Articles are what Muslims believe while Pillars are what Muslims do.
note_data.quickRecap: Six Articles of Faith are core beliefs: belief in Allah, angels, holy books, prophets, Day of Judgment, and predestination. Five Pillars are essential practices: declaration of faith, five daily prayers, charity, fasting during Ramadan, and pilgrimage to Mecca. Articles represent what Muslims believe, while Pillars represent what Muslims must do. Together they form the foundation of Islamic faith and practice.
